optimist.name: Candy Vanicek
optimist.column:
The loss stings, sure. But let’s not let a single game cloud the view of what’s brewing in this clubhouse. The Reds are still in the race, and that’s what matters. You can’t measure a season by one day, no matter how sweet the victory or how bitter the defeat. Elly De La Cruz? He’s the heartbeat of this team, and I don’t care what the scoreboard says. That boy’s got the kind of talent that makes you believe in the machine again.  

You see him, don’t you? The way he charges the bases like he’s got a personal vendetta against the entire league. Yesterday, he ran from first to third in a blink, and I swear I could hear Rose cussing from the beyond. This is Cincinnati baseball reborn. Sure, the Angels had their way with us, but that’s just the price of playing in a league full of giants. We’re not there yet, but we’re getting closer.  

The standings? Yeah, we’re a half-game behind. But let’s not forget how this team’s built. They’re young, they’re hungry, and they’re learning. Every loss is a lesson, and every win is a step forward. The front office? They’re laying the groundwork, and I’ll bet my chili sauce they’re already plotting the next move. You can’t build a contender overnight, but you can sure as hell start with the right pieces.  

Now, I know what some of you are thinking: “Candy, the pitching staff’s a mess!”

pessimist.name: Earl Huffnagle
pessimist.column:
The Reds lost again. Not in a dramatic, heart-wrenching way—no, this was the kind of loss that feels like a punchline delivered by a bored announcer. Los Angeles didn’t just beat us; they outplayed us, outmanaged us, and out-hustled us. Nine wins, seven losses. A half-game lead in the Central? Please. That’s the kind of math you’d see on a chalkboard in a third-grade classroom, if the teacher had a penchant for sarcasm.  

The problem isn’t the standings. The problem is the way we get there. Every year, we trade every star we develop. That’s the business model. The kids come up, they play great, they leave, and I drink and turn on the next one. It’s a cycle, sure, but it’s not a plan.
